name: Damu
description: Damu is a Sumerian deity associated with healing, medicine, and resurrection. He is considered a son of Enki (Ea), the god of wisdom and water, and Ninti (also known as Ninhursag), the mother goddess. Damu's primary role is to alleviate suffering and restore health to those afflicted by illness or injury. He possesses immense knowledge of medicinal herbs and healing practices, often depicted as a skilled physician. In some myths, Damu is also linked to vegetation and the cyclical renewal of life, mirroring his ability to bring life back from the brink of death. He is invoked in incantations and prayers for healing, and his presence is believed to bring comfort and restoration. Damu's connection to Enki grants him access to the divine waters, which are often used in healing rituals. He represents hope and the power of life to overcome adversity. While not as prominent as some other major Sumerian deities, Damu's importance lies in his specific role as a healer and restorer, essential for the well-being of the Sumerian people. He is a symbol of compassion and the divine ability to mend what is broken. His influence extends to both physical and spiritual healing, offering solace in times of sickness and despair. He is seen as a benevolent figure, dedicated to alleviating the pain and suffering of humankind.
subtitle: God of Healing and Resurrection

keyMyths.title: Damu's Birth
keyMyths.description: Damu is born to Enki and Ninti, inheriting his father's wisdom and his mother's nurturing qualities. His birth is celebrated as the arrival of a healer to the world.

keyMyths.title: Damu and the Sick
keyMyths.description: Damu travels across the land, using his knowledge of herbs and healing practices to cure the sick and wounded. He is revered for his compassion and skill.

keyMyths.title: Damu's Resurrection
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, Damu himself faces death but is resurrected by his parents, further solidifying his connection to the cycle of life and death. This event highlights his power over mortality.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
